meta.price_note: Typical street prices. Ranges [low,high] where vendors or batches differ. EUR prices converted approximately, marked in notes.

vga-rgb.name: Analog RGB
vga-rgb.explain: RGB for PVMs, SCART CRTs, upscalers - via a VGA connector or an AV-DIN breakout, board-dependent.

ypbpr.name: Component (YPbPr)
ypbpr.explain: Component video for consumer CRTs and some TVs.

yc.name: Y/C (S-Video)
yc.explain: Separated luma/chroma via MikeS11's encoder work. A usable, filtered Y/C signal, not just the raw source.

yc-active-filtered.name: Actively filtered Y/C
yc-active-filtered.explain: Y/C through a THS7374-class amplifier stage with NTSC-spec voltage correction, the cleanest chain.

yc-source.name: Y/C source signal
yc-source.explain: Internal token: a VGA-pin signal a Y/C adapter can filter. Provided by an analog I/O board or a Direct Video DAC.

yc-source-io.name: Y/C source from analog I/O board
yc-source-io.explain: Internal token: the MikeS11 ACTIVE adapter only accepts this (needs 5V on VGA pin 9, Sync-on-Green off); it rejects Direct Video sources.

sdram128.name: 128MB SDRAM
sdram128.explain: Core-side SDRAM. Needed by many cores; 128MB is the de facto standard (big Neo Geo ROMs, Mega CD, GBA).

dual-sdram.name: Dual 128MB SDRAM
dual-sdram.explain: Two modules; needed for Saturn and other dual-RAM cores.

sdram-slot.name: SDRAM socket
sdram-slot.explain: Internal token: accepts an SDRAM module.

gpio-header.name: GPIO expansion header
gpio-header.explain: Internal token: accepts the I/O-board addon stack.

user-io-port.name: USER I/O port
user-io-port.explain: Internal token: accepts SNAC controller adapters.

usb-otg-header.name: USB OTG header
usb-otg-header.explain: Internal token: accepts the USB hub addon.

usb-hub.name: Multiple USB ports
usb-hub.explain: Enough USB for controllers, keyboard, WiFi, etc. without a squid of adapters.

ethernet.name: Wired Ethernet
ethernet.explain: For updates, game streaming from NAS, remote control.

snac.name: SNAC (native controller ports)
snac.explain: Original console controllers wired near-directly into the core; lowest possible input lag.

nfc.name: NFC game loading
nfc.explain: Tap a card/figure to launch a game (Zaparoo-style).

cd-drive.name: Optical disc drive
cd-drive.explain: Load real CDs (PS1 now; Saturn/Sega CD support maturing in firmware).

m2-slot.name: M.2 SSD bay
m2-slot.explain: Internal storage beyond the SD card.

jamma.name: JAMMA edge
jamma.explain: Drops into a real arcade cabinet as the game PCB.

usbc-power.name: USB-C power
usbc-power.explain: Powered by any decent USB-C phone charger; no barrel brick.

barrel-power.name: 5V barrel-jack power
barrel-power.explain: Internal token: needs a 5V DC barrel supply.

boot-media.name: Boot SD card
boot-media.explain: Internal token: SD card with the MiSTer OS.

psu.name: Power supply
psu.explain: Internal token: an adequate power source.

case.name: Enclosure
case.explain: Internal token: a case around the board stack.

controller.name: A controller
controller.explain: Internal token: any USB gamepad to actually play.

midi-mt32.name: MT-32 MIDI synth
midi-mt32.explain: Roland MT-32/SoundFont music for DOS-era cores via mt32-pi.

handheld.name: Handheld
handheld.explain: A MiSTer you can hold. Nothing orderable provides this yet.

lanes.name: Turnkey consoles
lanes.tagline: Plug in and play. No screwdriver.

lanes.name: Assembled kits
lanes.tagline: The classic stack, built and tested by someone else.

lanes.name: Build your own
lanes.tagline: A bare board plus the addons you choose.

lanes.name: Special builds
lanes.tagline: Cabinets and consoles with their own boards.

lanes.name: On the horizon
lanes.tagline: Announced. Not orderable. Do not wait for these unless you enjoy waiting.

sdram-128.name: 128MB SDRAM module
sdram-128.notes: The de facto standard module; 32MB is largely phased out of retail. Many popular cores need SDRAM at all, so treat this as part of a real build, not an extra.

sdram-128-second.name: Second 128MB SDRAM module (dual RAM)
sdram-128-second.notes: Dual-RAM configs (Saturn, future PSX work) need two modules and an I/O board that supports the second socket (e.g. IO Direct).

io-analog.name: Analog I/O board, VGA-class (official v6 / A/V Pro 9.2)
io-analog.vendor: Retro Remake A/V Pro 9.2 ($40) / official v6-class boards (~$60)
io-analog.notes: The VGA-connector generation of analog I/O boards: RGB or YPbPr by cable, analog audio, and the VGA pins the MikeS11 Y/C adapters plug into. Distinct from MiSTer Addons IO Analog Pro (own entry), which has no VGA port. A EUR349.90 price seen in one search result is a stale-snippet artifact. Retro Remake A/V Pro 9.2 sold out at review 2026-07-16.

io-analog-pro.name: MiSTer Addons IO Analog Pro
io-analog-pro.notes: Current-generation analog board: RGBs (correct 700mV), YPbPr, and NATIVE S-Video/composite (MikeS Y/C core support) through an ADV7125 24-bit DAC and a Saturn Mini-DIN-10 connector - no VGA port, so MikeS11 dongles do not apply to it. HDMI stays live simultaneously. In stock at review (verified on the product page 2026-07-16). Whether the onboard Y/C path is actively filtered to MikeS11-Active standard is the same open question as the SuperStation One.

reflex-prism.name: MiSTer Addons Reflex Prism (HDMI to analog)
reflex-prism.notes: Lag-free 1:1 digital-to-analog converter for Direct Video (direct_video=2, 15kHz modes): HD15 port does RGB or YPbPr (switch, up to 1920x1200), Saturn Mini-DIN-10 does RGB and native S-Video simultaneously. Composite is present but the vendor itself calls it unsupported "due to dot crawl" - honest, so we do not claim it. $80 assembled V1.1, ready to ship at review (an earlier $40 figure from a collection listing was wrong).

io-digital.name: Digital I/O board (v1.2)
io-digital.notes: S/PDIF digital audio out (Mini-TOSLINK + TOSLINK), second MicroSD slot, onboard power switch; DE10-Nano SW3 must be ON. Price verified on the live product page 2026-07-16 (the earlier EUR349.90 snippet was a stale artifact, as suspected).

usb-hub.name: USB hub addon (v2.1)
usb-hub.notes: 7 ports (1 power-only + 6 USB 2.0), FE2.1 chip; needs its own power via rear barrel splitter (included).

direct-video-dac.name: Direct Video HDMI DAC (generic AG6200-class dongle)
direct-video-dac.notes: Firmware feature (direct_video=1) plus a cheap HDMI-to-VGA dongle: analog RGB without an I/O board, same low latency. While enabled the HDMI port stops being normal HDMI. Some gear wants a 470-ohm inline resistor on sync.

yc-passive.name: MikeS11 Y/C adapter, PASSIVE
yc-passive.notes: Passive luma-trap filter, no amplifier. Explicitly dual-compatible: analog I/O board VGA (direct_video=0) OR Direct Video DAC (direct_video=1). The flexible budget path to S-Video.

yc-active.name: MikeS11 Y/C adapter, ACTIVE (THS7374)
yc-active.vendor: Ultimate MiSTer (EUR24.90 ~ $27); MiSTer Addons YC Active Encoder Board ($55)
yc-active.notes: THS7374 amplifier, NTSC-spec voltage modes, luma trap for clean composite. The Direct Video question is a DOCUMENTED contradiction between two primary sources (both verified 2026-07-16): the official MiSTer CRT guide (MkDocs, co-written by MikeS11) gives a Direct Video configuration section stating "Active / Passive adapters will include a SOG circuit" - no active-adapter restriction - while Ultimate MiSTer's SKU page states "This adapter is not compatible with Direct Video HDMI-to-VGA dongles" and requires 5V on VGA pin 9 via the analog I/O board's jumper. We model the restrictive reading because it is the shipping SKU's own spec and the physical rationale is plain: the active amp draws its 5V from pin 9, which a bare DV dongle does not supply. EARMARK 2026-07-16: site owner recalls Y/C over a DV dongle working - fully consistent with the PASSIVE adapter, which is documented Direct-Video-compatible everywhere.

snac-adapter.name: SNAC / SNAX / SNAX64 adapter
snac-adapter.vendor: MiSTer Addons ($15 SNAC V1, $35 SNAX V2.1 adds PSX/NeoGeo+2P, $50 SNAX64 adds 4P N64); Retro Remake bundles $5-10
snac-adapter.notes: Plugs into the USER I/O header, not the I/O board. PSX and N64 controllers need SNAX/SNAX64 tiers, not SNAC V1. Console cables usually sold separately. USB alternatives that keep original pads without SNAC wiring: MiSTer Addons Reflex Adapt (\, GP2040-CE, ~1ms) and the Bliss-Box family (Ultimate MiSTer's BlisSTer hub tier integrates it) - accessories, so they are notes here, not entries.

nfc-reader.name: NFC reader (Zaparoo/TapTo launching)
nfc-reader.vendor: Retro Remake NFC Reader Pack (\, in stock) and generic USB readers
nfc-reader.notes: Tap-a-card game launching via the Zaparoo (ex-TapTo) ecosystem; plugs into any USB port, so any option here can add it. The SuperStation One ships one built in.

rtc-board.name: RTC board
rtc-board.notes: Cheap, niche: few cores use it, auto-set anyway if networked.

mt32-pi-kit.name: MT32-pi interface kit
mt32-pi-kit.vendor: various (~$24 for the interface; Raspberry Pi 3+ sold separately)
mt32-pi-kit.notes: Roland MT-32 / SoundFont synth for DOS-era cores; budget the Pi on top.

case-armor.name: Aluminum Armor case (passive cooling)
case-armor.notes: Whole case is the heatsink; fanless. Fits DE10-Nano-class boards incl. MiSTer Pi with an IO Analog Pro / IO Direct board.

case-acrylic.name: Acrylic case
case-acrylic.notes: Cheapest enclosure; vented for a fan.

psu-barrel-5a.name: 5V 5A barrel power supply
psu-barrel-5a.notes: The recommended upgrade over stock 2A-class adapters once addons hang off the board.

sd-card.name: MicroSD card (32GB+, U1/Class 10)
sd-card.notes: SanDisk Ultra class is the community default.

usb-controller.name: a USB gamepad
usb-controller.notes: 8BitDo-class pads are the community default; Bluetooth works where the option provides it.

superdock.name: SuperDock
superdock.notes: SuperStation One only. Tray-loading DVD-RW, unpopulated M.2 2280 bay, 4 more USB-A, IR remote. Preorder, sold out; disc loading works for PS1, Saturn/Sega CD support still maturing in firmware. Shipped later than the original window.

options.name: Retro Remake SuperStation One
options.notes: PSone-shaped complete MiSTer: 128MB RAM, ADV7125 24-bit DAC, HDMI up to 1440p-class, VGA/component/composite/S-Video plus a Saturn-LOOKING 10-pin mini-DIN that is NOT pin-compatible with real Saturn cables. PS1 SNAC controller+memory-card ports, NFC loading, wired Ethernet (vendor Ports spec, verified 2026-07-16; cnx-software corroborates the RJ45), WiFi/BT, 64GB card preinstalled. Y/C is native, no dongle needed; DIP switch 3 selects the FPGA MikeS-style encoder vs an onboard Sony encoder chip - RetroRGB: "Put them in the Up/FPGA position and MiSTer generates Composite and S-Video via the MikeS FPGA code"; forum consensus calls the Sony path "not as good". Whether the FPGA path is THS7374-class actively filtered remains UNVERIFIED by any teardown. Early firmware had mono/color-banding glitches on some cores, since patched. RTC needs a user-installed CR1220. No PSU in box. Sells in batches; sold out between batches (Batch 4 gone as of July 2026).
options.what: A complete MiSTer in a PSone-style shell: plug it into power and a TV and you are playing.
options.advice: The most talked-about turnkey option right now. Batches sell out, so getting one takes patience. Original PS1 pads and memory cards plug straight in, and the S-Video/composite output needs no extra parts.

options.name: MiSTer Addons Aluminum Armor Bundle
options.notes: The classic full stack in the fanless aluminum case, assembled and tested. I/O board chosen at purchase: IO Analog Pro (1x SDRAM, simultaneous HDMI+analog, native S-Video/composite) or IO Direct (2x SDRAM, HDMI or analog). 32GB card preloaded. Made to order; both configs sold out at review ("back in soon"), backorders final. $475 regular price (an earlier $425 figure is stale). A "Skeleton" caseless bundle that once appeared in search results is no longer a live product - its URL serves this bundle.
options.what: The classic MiSTer stack - board, RAM, I/O, USB hub - pre-assembled in a silent all-aluminum case.
options.advice: A commonly recommended one-box route to the traditional stack, with S-Video and RGB out of the box. Pick the IO Direct configuration if Saturn-class cores matter to you (it ships dual RAM).

variants.name: IO Direct configuration
variants.note: Ships 2x 128MB SDRAM for Saturn-class cores; HDMI or analog rather than both at once.

options.name: Ultimate MiSTer PRO
options.notes: EUR349.90 excl VAT. Genuine DE10-Nano (explicitly not a clone), AV Pro 9.2 board, official case, assembled and tested. DUAL RAM tier at ~EUR379.90 adds second module + Digital I/O + 24-bit Direct Video for Saturn-class cores; BlisSTer tier (~EUR419.90) swaps in the low-latency BlisSTer hub, HDMI-only, no VGA.
options.what: A pre-built classic stack on a genuine DE10-Nano, assembled and tested in the EU.
options.advice: A long-standing, commonly recommended EU route to the traditional stack, with VGA analog out as standard. The DUAL RAM tier is the one to pick for Saturn-class cores.

variants.name: DUAL RAM tier
variants.note: Second 128MB module, Digital I/O and 24-bit Direct Video for Saturn-class cores (EUR379.90).

variants.name: BlisSTer tier
variants.note: Low-latency BlisSTer hub with native gamepad ports; HDMI-only, no VGA (EUR419.90).

options.name: QMTech Factory-Assembled MiSTer
options.vendor: QMTech (AliExpress); resold by Ultimate MiSTer as "Ultimate MiSTer FPGA"
options.notes: Factory-assembled QMTech MiSTer, specs from the AliExpress listing (supplied by the site owner 2026-07-16): QMTech Cyclone V SoC board with 128MB SDRAM built in, QMTech Official V2.1 IO board (24-bit VGA DAC, I2S audio via 3.5mm, SNAC, 4-port USB hub, RTC - CR1220 battery not included), PCB-material case, 32GB TF card, USB cable, EU or US 5V PSU. Ultimate MiSTer resells this class of build at EUR199.90 (out of stock at review). Priced $159 per RetroRGB (Oct 2024) for this exact bundle - a dated anchor, not a verified current price; AliExpress prices drift and vary by region (US listings reportedly cheaper than international), and their pages block automated verification. Whether the V2.1 IO board hosts MikeS11 ACTIVE adapters (5V on VGA pin 9) is unverified; the passive chain applies regardless.
options.what: A factory-built MiSTer on the QMTech board - the lowest-priced ready-made option.
options.advice: A popular budget entry. The trade-offs are AliExpress shipping and less hand-holding than the boutique kits; the hardware itself covers the essentials, SNAC and RTC included.

options.name: iCode Fully Built MiSTer
options.notes: In stock at check, which is rare in this market. Price tiers by SD size (64GB-1TB). Digital I/O build: no analog video out of the box. 6A supply, RGB fan, RTC board loose in box. Dual-RAM upgrade available.
options.what: A fully assembled DE10-Nano build with digital audio I/O, sold from US stock.
options.advice: One of the few builds reliably in stock. It has no analog video output as configured, so it suits modern-TV setups best - CRT paths need added parts (see the fine print).

options.name: RetroCastle Full Kit
options.notes: Premium Chinese full-kit line on a genuine DE10-Nano, four variants at review (browser-verified storefront 2026-07-16): VGA 2023 \, Saturn 2023 \, Digital Dual-SDRAM 2024 \, Saturn Dual-SDRAM 2024 \. Saturn versions use a DIN-style analog connector, dual-SDRAM versions target Saturn/PSX-class cores; exact box contents per variant not yet itemized here - treat includes as the conservative common core (board + one 128MB module) pending a per-variant sweep. Also sells IO boards, metal cases, PSX SNAC gear.
options.what: Premium pre-built kits on a genuine DE10-Nano, sold in four configurations.
options.advice: The high end of the pre-built market. The dual-SDRAM 2024 versions aim at Saturn-class cores; the Saturn-connector versions suit CRT setups.

variants.name: Saturn 2023 version
variants.note: Saturn-style AV connector instead of VGA.

variants.name: Saturn Dual-SDRAM 2024 version
variants.note: Dual RAM plus the Saturn AV connector.

options.name: Terasic DE10-Nano
options.notes: The reference board. $225 direct from Terasic, which restocks regularly (site owner observation 2026-07-16; the "chronic scarcity" framing came from an undated shortage-era buying guide). Third-party resellers mark it up when they have it at all. Ships a 5V/2.5A adapter; upgrade advised once addons stack up.
options.what: The original reference board every MiSTer descends from. Bare, it can boot a handful of cores over HDMI - but almost nobody uses one that way.
options.advice: Most newcomers now start with a MiSTer Pi or a pre-built kit; the DE10-Nano remains the pick if you want the reference hardware itself, and everything in the ecosystem fits it.

options.name: Retro Remake MiSTer Pi
options.notes: The board that broke the DE10 price. Same Cyclone V class, 1GB DDR3, USB-C power, accessory-compatible dimensions/headers. Sold in preorder batches; board listed From $125 and SOLD OUT at review 2026-07-16, with no discontinuation or final-batch language on the store - accessories (SDRAM $20, hub $20, acrylic case $10, SNAC bundles) mostly remain in stock, A/V Pro 9.2 and RTC sold out. Watch for batch 6 or an end-of-line signal; Retro Remake's focus is visibly the SuperStation One.
options.what: A modern remake of the reference board: same FPGA class, USB-C power, sold in batches.
options.advice: The most commonly recommended starting point for self-builders. It sells out between batches, and its accessory line (RAM, hub, case) is inexpensive.

options.name: QMTech Cyclone V Clone
options.notes: Automotive-grade Cyclone V with a higher thermal ceiling than the DE10's chip: the fanless builder's pick, and actually in stock when others aren't. Some variants have SDRAM integrated on-board (+~$15; then skip the module). Caveats: RAM slot orientation differs from DE10 on one header (people have fried modules inserting them the DE10 way) and QC is more variable than Terasic. Official sales channel is the QMTech AliExpress store; the linked listing was supplied by the site owner 2026-07-16 and cannot be machine-verified (AliExpress blocks automated access) - AliExpress URLs are volatile, re-check on each sweep.
options.what: The Chinese clone of the reference board, often with the 128MB RAM already soldered on.
options.advice: Liked for being in stock when others are not, and by fanless builders for its thermal headroom. Quality control varies more than the originals - mind the RAM-slot orientation note before assembling.

options.name: MiSTer Addons MiSTercade V2
options.notes: Your MiSTer becomes the JAMMA PCB in a real cabinet: 24-bit video via dedicated RGB DAC, adjustable 3.3V JAMMA edge, 13.5W mono amp, GP2040-CE sub-1ms controls, 8 buttons/player. This entry is the Full Kit at $550 (DE10 + dual RAM included); the $275 shown on the kits collection is the Lite tier (BYO board + RAM). Sold out at review ("back in soon"). Cabinet not included, obviously. The Versus 2P sidekick board (\) is deliberately not listed: accessory, not a starting point.
options.what: A kit that turns a MiSTer into the JAMMA board inside a real arcade cabinet.
options.advice: The standard answer for cabinet builds. The full kit includes the board and dual RAM; the Lite tier suits people who already own a MiSTer.

options.name: Heber MiSTer Multisystem2
options.notes: Not a DE10 carrier: one integrated motherboard (own Cyclone V, 128MB SDRAM + 1GB LPDDR, powered 7-port hub) in a 3D-printed enclosure, assembled. Digital version GBP180 ex VAT (~$235, HDMI only); Analogue version GBP220 ex VAT (~$285) adds 24-bit analog out: 9-pin mini-DIN RGB+C-sync for SCART AND a 15-way VGA-style port for CRT/arcade monitors (vendor spec verified 2026-07-16; S-Video/composite are NOT native - the DIN carries RGB). Whether the VGA port hosts MikeS11 Y/C adapters is untested - it should carry the standard MiSTer analog signal, worth verifying. In stock, 1-2 week lead. JAMMA Arcade edition preorders ship from Aug 2026. Enclosure files are open (Printables, CC-BY-SA).
options.what: A complete console built around its own integrated MiSTer board, assembled in the UK.
options.advice: The turnkey option that is actually in stock. CRT owners usually go for the Analogue version; the Digital version is the cheaper HDMI-only route.

variants.name: Analogue version
variants.note: 24-bit analog out: mini-DIN RGB for SCART plus a VGA-style port. The base (Digital) version is HDMI-only.

options.name: d3fmod Ironclad DX (Mini-ITX)
options.notes: A Mini-ITX carrier that turns any ITX/ATX PC case into a MiSTer: your DE10-Nano, MiSTer Pi or QMTech Cyclone V drops onto it (complete price below assumes a MiSTer Pi; the PC case itself is also yours to supply). 24-bit Analog Devices DAC with a Saturn-style RGB/S-Video connector and an INTERNAL PASSIVE LUMA TRAP - native Y/C with vendor-documented filtering (composite needs a cable adapter, not claimed here). Dual SDRAM (official v9.2-compliant), 7 USB, SNAC via DB9 + USB3 user port, RTC, i2s audio DAC + headphone amp, tape in, IR remote power, fan control keyed to FPGA temperature, over-voltage/short protection. EUR219.99 (~USD240); hand-assembled in monthly batches, out of stock at review 2026-07-16. Successor to the Ironclad Plus.
options.what: A carrier board that turns a Mini-ITX PC case into a MiSTer, with CRT outputs and smart power built in.
options.advice: A favorite among PC-case builders - S-Video is built in and documented. Hand-made in small monthly batches, so catching stock is the hard part. You supply the FPGA board (DE10, MiSTer Pi or QMTech) and the case.

options.name: Heber MiSTer Multisystem (1)
options.notes: The original 2021-era Multisystem: a carrier board + console case you installed your own DE10-Nano into (a screwdriver product, not turnkey). Discontinued as a Heber product and released as fully open-source hardware (schematics and PCB files on GitHub, 2023); superseded by the integrated Multisystem2. Listed so the landscape says what happened to it: you can still build one, you can no longer buy one.
options.what: The original Multisystem console: a case and carrier board your DE10-Nano dropped into.
options.advice: No longer sold; the design is open-source now and the Multisystem2 is its successor. Listed so you know where it went.

options.name: Taki Udon Agilex 5 boards ('MiSTer 2')
options.vendor: Taki Udon / Retro Remake, with Altera
options.notes: Engineering boards in MiSTer Pi form factor being seeded to developers; no consumer product or date. Stated target 1.5-3x MiSTer Pi pricing. Motivation: Cyclone V is out of headroom for full N64/Saturn accuracy; Agilex 5 could reach toward Dreamcast-class. Cores must be ported before any of this plays games.
options.what: The next-generation FPGA boards Taki Udon is developing on Altera's Agilex 5 - what the community calls 'MiSTer 2'.
options.advice: Developer seeding only so far, no product or date. Buy for what exists today; treat this as the future, not an option.

options.name: Heber Multisystem2 Pocket
options.notes: Handheld Multisystem2 in open community-input development; 4:3 screen, modular controls planned; 'before end of 2026' aspiration, no committed date or price. The widescreen 'Wide Boy' prototype seen in press is explicitly NOT the final design. Only credible MiSTer handheld project found; no orderable MiSTer portable exists.
options.what: A handheld take on the Multisystem2, being designed in the open with community input.
options.advice: Aiming for late 2026. Nothing to order yet - and no orderable MiSTer handheld exists from anyone.

options.name: Terasic DE25-Nano
options.notes: The board is real and orderable ($248; ~25% more logic, DDR4, much faster ARM) but MiSTer does NOT run on it: no official framework port confirmed as of July 2026. Listed so nobody buys one expecting a MiSTer. A likely future host, nothing more yet.
options.what: Terasic's successor to the DE10-Nano: real and orderable, but MiSTer does not run on it yet.
options.advice: No framework port exists as of mid-2026. Do not buy one expecting a MiSTer; watch this space instead.

options.name: MARS FPGA
options.notes: CANCELLED November 2025. Was pitched as a DE10-eclipsing open board (Dreamcast/NAOMI/3DO/Jaguar ambitions, 4K + full analog out). Kept here so the landscape says so out loud instead of letting old hype linger.
options.what: A once-announced next-generation board with big ambitions, cancelled in late 2025.
options.advice: Listed so the landscape says plainly: it is not coming.

appendix_not_mister.name: Analogue Pocket / Analogue 3D
appendix_not_mister.one_liner: Closed platform with its own 'openFPGA' core format; polished, but not MiSTer's cores, updates, or ecosystem (the 3D is N64-only, no openFPGA).

appendix_not_mister.name: ModRetro Chromatic
appendix_not_mister.one_liner: A single-system FPGA Game Boy/GBC replica ($199+), cartridge-based; no relation to MiSTer.

appendix_not_mister.name: ModRetro M64
appendix_not_mister.one_liner: A dedicated N64-compatible FPGA console ($229.99, launching July 28, 2026, AMD Artix UltraScale+, real cartridges); single-system, not MiSTer.

appendix_not_mister.name: FPGBC (FunnyPlaying)
appendix_not_mister.one_liner: A budget FPGA Game Boy Color clone handheld/kit; single-system, not MiSTer.

appendix_not_mister.name: Replay2 (FPGAArcade)
appendix_not_mister.one_liner: An in-development Agilex 5 mini-ITX FPGA machine from the FPGAArcade lineage; same next-gen conversation, different core ecosystem, not MiSTer-compatible.

interview.q: How much building do you want to do?

answers.label: None - ready out of the box
I want to build it myself

interview.q: What are you plugging it into?
Original console controllers, plugged in natively?

answers.label: A shelf, a desk, under the TV
